author: @williamarmstrong7199
text: I have always wanted to mix some uv reactive salts into glass that is not UV blocking (to led UV frequencies) then make artwork out of the moulton glass that changes its appearance based on the frequency of the light shining through it.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: That is a super cool idea! give it ago. cheers mate! :)

author: @NorthernKitty
text:
I hadn't thought of Sharpies and Dry Erase Markers with a coating of acrylic spray!!  That probably would've saved me a bit of money over the $30 set of Gallery Glass paints I purchased for this purpose!!  Great tip!!
One thing you might try that's a really neat effect... if you frost the opposite surface behind the areas you "painted", and then have hidden LED's shining perpendicular to the edge of the glass, the light passing through the glass will refract when it hits the frosted portion, lighting up the area behind the colored areas to make them glow.  It works best if the edges of the glass are polished.  (Basically, this is using the same technique employed for backlighting LCD monitors.)

author: @Twistedmetal-qe8kx

author: @ericvonessex9353
text:
Fantastic video as usual; always informative and fascinating, many thanks I've learnt a lot from you and it's much appreciated. Similar to yourself and Robert, I've always made and repaired things.

Your printing on wood was especially interesting as I've tried various techniques and failed. Your idea of using varnish and especially soaking the print in water first was the breakthrough needed! There are various projects where I want to print on wood, such as a small garden memorial for my late parents and putting  art nouveau illustrations onto my home made furniture. Your video has really helped me.

I've always been fascinated with sundials. A few years back I made a equinoctial sundial from an old Ikea lampshade. Sadly it hasn't stood the test of time as despite several coats of paint and varnish the plastic has given up! I'm now planning to construct a conventional horizontal sundial, cast from concrete. Making the mould isn't as straightforward as it first seems. A large plastic flowerpot saucer will be be the basis of the mould. The problem comes when adding the roman numerals. They can be purchased but I prefer to make them from matchsticks. Gluing them back to front at the bottom of the flowerpot saucer before pouring the concrete mix may prove to be a very fiddly job! The glue may not hold.  I could cast the sundial and add the Roman numerals, made from thin strips of old ceramic tiles I have laying around, whilst the mix is still wet. There are plusses and minuses with both techniques. Haven't made my mind up yet which one to go for.  I wonder if you or Robert will be making a sundial? Especially as spring and summer are approaching.

Keep up the good work and I look forward to your future projects.

author: @theloveiswhere
text: I use piping bags for black - take small squares of plastic and roll them to form a cone. Fill and tape closed, then cut tip off.
